edge: The work addresses cross-view camera pose estimation by determining the camera pose of a ground-level image with respect to an aerial image of the local area.
SliceMatch consists of ground and aerial feature extractors for feature extraction from ground and aerial images.
The feature aggregators construct a ground descriptor and multiple pose-dependent aerial descriptors from candidate camera poses.
The novel aerial feature aggregator includes a cross-view attention module for ground-view guided aerial feature selection.
The aerial feature aggregator utilizes the geometric projection of the ground camera's viewing frustum on the aerial image to pool features.
SliceMatch is trained using contrastive learning and pose estimation is formulated as a similarity comparison between ground and aerial descriptors.
SliceMatch achieves a 19% lower median localization error on the VIGOR benchmark compared to the state-of-the-art using VGG16 backbone.
When using a ResNet50 backbone, SliceMatch achieves a 50% lower error compared to the state-of-the-art.
Cross-view camera pose estimation aims to estimate the 3-Degrees-of-Freedom ground camera pose by comparing the captured images.
The 3-DoF ground camera pose is related to planar location and orientation as it involves determining the camera's position and direction on a two-dimensional plane.
The ground-level image is compared to the geo-referenced aerial image to estimate the camera's pose and its local surroundings.
The local aerial image can be obtained from a reference database to improve localization accuracy.
GNSS and image retrieval can be used as rough localization priors for obtaining the local aerial image.
The sources of error, including GNSS, can lead to inaccuracies in the localization process.
The cross-view formulation offers an alternative approach to ground camera pose estimation compared to traditional techniques.
SliceMatch is a method used to identify the ground-level camera pose within an aerial image by dividing the horizontal field-of-view into slices and applying self-attention.
Slice-specific aerial feature maps are used for ground slice features aggregation to create a dense scoring map for camera pose estimation.
Pose-dependent aerial slice masks enable the aggregation of candidate poses for efficient testing and comparison with ground descriptors.
Techniques for ground-level camera pose estimation may require detailed 3D point cloud maps or semantic maps for accurate results.

3D point cloud maps and semantic maps are both types of maps used for representing Earth's surface, with 3D point cloud maps providing detailed point information and semantic maps providing labeled information.
Aerial imagery provides continuous coverage of Earth's surface, making it easier to collect accurate point clouds or semantic maps.

Global image descriptor-based methods and dense pixel-level feature-based methods are two categories of methods used for cross-view camera localization and pose estimation, with the former focusing on compact image representation and fast inference time and the latter on preserving more details and using geometric relationships between views for accurate estimation.
The global descriptor-based approach is improved in this work by enforcing feature locality in the descriptor, aiming for accurate and efficient camera pose estimation.

node_2: geometry of the ground camera’s viewing frustum
edge: The slice mask P k,n expresses the geometry of the ground camera’s viewing frustum in the aerial feature map for the n-th orientation slice, assuming that the camera would have the k-th pose.
Each cell in the slice mask contains a value in the range [0, 1] proportional to how much of that cell intersects the frustum.
With the slice masks, the n-th aerial slice descriptor at pose k can be computed efficiently.
For each of the C channels, we compute a weighted average over all of the L × L spatial locations (i, j) in the feature map z′n a, using the elements of slice mask P k,n as weights.
